title: Disaster Planning
description: If-Then plans convert coping decisions into pre-programmed responses.

blocks.content: The athletes who look unbreakable on race day are not the ones who avoided problems. They are the ones who solved their problems six weeks early. Mile 47 of a 100-mile race — stomach turns, nausea hits like a wall. If this is the first time you're encountering it, you'll burn enormous cognitive resources solving it in real-time. But what if you'd already decided: 'Switch to water and broth, walk five minutes, reassess'? No decision to make. Only execution.

Only imagining success is like only training for flat courses and then showing up to a race with 10,000 feet of climbing. The best athletes aren't unshakeable because nothing goes wrong. They're unshakeable because they've already been there in their minds — rehearsed the disaster, felt the stomach turn, and practiced responding calmly, specifically, and automatically. The disaster isn't new. It's a drill.
By the end of this lesson, you will have 5 specific If-Then plans for your most likely race-day problems.

blocks.question: Retrieval from Lesson 3.2: The Highlight Reel has 3 parts. Which is the FIRST?
blocks.explanation: Correct. The reel starts with an empowering past memory — your anchor. Evidence-based confidence from something that actually happened.

blocks.content: If-Then plans convert coping decisions into pre-programmed responses — eliminating the cognitive cost of in-race deliberation. You've already decided. You just execute.

items.title: Gollwitzer & Sheeran (2006) — Implementation Intentions
items.content: The landmark meta-analysis covered 94 studies and over 8,000 participants. Effect size d = .65 — medium-to-large. People with specific if-then plans were significantly more likely to follow through than those who simply set goals. The conclusion is unambiguous: pre-planning your response to obstacles works.

items.title: Process vs. Outcome — Coping Imagery
items.content: Hanton & Jones (1999) used coping imagery with elite swimmers — mentally rehearsing adverse scenarios and successful responses. Athletes showed reduced debilitative anxiety AND improved performance. When the bad moment came, it wasn't new. It was a drill they'd run before.

items.title: Specificity Matters
items.content: Tamminen & Holt (2012) found athletes with pre-planned coping responses handled in-race adversity better than improvisers. The improvisers weren't less tough — they were burning more cognitive resources on the same problem. Decision fatigue is real, and every in-race choice drains the same prefrontal cortex managing your effort and emotions.

blocks.prompt: Sort each If-Then plan into 'Effective' or 'Too Vague':

items.text: If my stomach cramps at mile 50, I will switch to water only and walk for 2 minutes.
If things go wrong, I'll deal with it.
If I get dropped on the first climb, I will hold my pace, use my pain response statement, and ride my own race.

feedback.correct: Correct. Effective If-Then plans specify both the trigger AND the response in concrete, actionable terms. 'I'll deal with it' and 'I'll adjust' require in-race deliberation — exactly what If-Then plans are designed to eliminate.
feedback.incorrect: Review the specificity principle. An effective If-Then plan names the exact trigger and the exact response. Vague plans like 'deal with it' still require decision-making under pressure.

items.label: List your 5 most likely race-day problems
items.detail: Not far-fetched disasters — the LIKELY ones. Physical (cramping, GI, bonking). Mechanical (flats, equipment). Environmental (heat, wind). Tactical (too fast, dropped). Psychological (doubt spiral, wanting to quit). Your five.

items.label: Write specific If-Then responses
items.detail: "If [specific trigger], then I will [specific action]." Not vague. Not 'I'll figure it out.' Example: 'If I get dropped, I will reset to sustainable effort, focus on the next checkpoint only, and stop looking behind me.'

items.label: Visualize each trigger-response in first person
items.detail: Close your eyes. See the cramp hitting, the stomach turning, the competitor attacking. Then see yourself executing your pre-planned response — calmly, automatically, like a drill you've run a hundred times.

items.label: Rehearse until automatic
items.detail: Review your 5 plans before every key session and race. Read them out loud. After each race, audit: did the scenario happen? Did the if-then work? Update, refine, replace.

blocks.content: Your commitment: I will have 5 complete If-Then plans written and rehearsed by two weeks before my next race: _______________.
Hope is not a race strategy. Preparation is.
Next — the soundtrack running in your head. You're going to talk to yourself during the race anyway. Time to decide what you say.
